1.1: Amos prophesied ca. 760-750 BCE in the northern kingdom under Jeroboam II, a period of remarkable prosperity and military success — and of corresponding social injustice and covenant neglect. Amos is the first of the classical writing prophets and the OT's most sustained advocate for social justice: the Day of the LORD will expose the oppression of the poor (5:11-12), the corruption of the courts (5:12), the religious hypocrisy of the sanctuaries (5:21-24: I hate, I despise your feasts ... let justice roll down like waters). His social critique is grounded in covenant theology: YHWH's covenant with Israel demands covenant justice in the community; religious observance without ethical faithfulness is an abomination.
1.2: The opening roar-oracle positions Amos's prophecy as YHWH speaking from Zion (the southern capital, Jerusalem) to the northern kingdom. The lion-roar formula (YHWH mishion yish'ag) establishes divine authority: the prophetic word is YHWH's own declaration. Carmel, the fertile ridge overlooking the Jezreel Valley, withering at YHWH's voice indicates judgment reaching into Israel's most productive agricultural land — the very prosperity that Jeroboam II's era had produced is threatened.
1.3: The 'three crimes / four' formula (al sheloshah pisha'ei / ve'al arba'ah lo ashivenu) is a graded-number idiom common in ANE wisdom literature (cf. Prov 30:15-33: 'three things... four'), used here as a rhetorical device for the accumulation of guilt: the fourth transgression crosses a threshold that triggers YHWH's irreversible judgment. Damascus (Aram) was Israel's most persistent northern enemy; the Aramean wars of the 9th-8th centuries BCE are documented in the Tel Dan inscription and Assyrian records. 'Threshing Gilead with iron threshing sledges' refers to documented Aramean military atrocities in Transjordanian Israel (cf. 2 Kgs 10:32-33).
1.4: Ben-hadad was a dynastic title for Aramean kings of Damascus (Ben-hadad I, II, III are attested in the OT and in the Assyrian annals of Shalmaneser III). Hazael, his predecessor, was the Aramean king whose dynasty Ben-hadad II inherited — confirmed in Assyrian records as the one Shalmaneser III fought at Qarqar (853 BCE). The 'house of Hazael' and 'fortresses of Ben-hadad' are the specific political and military infrastructure of the Aramean state that YHWH's judgment targets.
1.5: The Valley of Aven (probably the Beqa'a Valley in modern Lebanon) and Beth-eden (possibly Bit-adini, an Aramean state on the middle Euphrates) identify specific administrative centers of the Aramean power structure. 'The ruler from the Valley of Aven and the scepter-bearer of Beth-eden' are the regional vassal governors. The threat that the Syrian people will go into exile 'to Kir' is fulfilled in 2 Kings 16:9, when the Assyrian king Tiglath-pileser III conquered Damascus and deported Aram's population to Kir — precise historical fulfillment of an 8th-century prophecy.
1.6: Gaza was the southernmost of the five Philistine cities, strategically located on the main coastal trade route between Egypt and the Levant (the Via Maris). The crime specified — taking an entire community into exile to sell to Edom — refers to the slave trade that operated through Philistia. Gaza's role as a slave-trading hub is confirmed in Joel 3:4-8, which describes Philistines and Phoenicians selling Judeans to Greeks. The 'complete exile' (galut shlemah) delivered to Edom implies total depopulation of a community — a war crime under the ancient Near Eastern understanding of limits on warfare.
1.7: The 'wall of Gaza' is the fortified perimeter of the city itself; the 'fortresses' (armon) are the palace-citadels where the ruling class lived and stored wealth. The burning of the fortresses as YHWH's judgment follows the standard pattern of ANE military destruction — conquest was typically expressed in fire and demolition of elite structures. Gaza was subsequently conquered by Assyrians under Tiglath-pileser III (734 BCE), who records Gaza's king Hanunu fleeing to Egypt.
1.8: Ashdod, Ashkelon, and Ekron were three of the five Philistine cities, along with Gaza (v7) and Gath (which Amos notably omits here, probably because it had already been destroyed by Hazael of Aram ca. 815 BCE, 2 Kgs 12:17). The 'remnant of the Philistines' — those who survive the judgment — reflects Amos's awareness that the Philistine city-states would be substantially destroyed rather than completely eradicated. Assyrian records confirm the subjugation of Philistia in the late 8th century, with Ashdod being destroyed by Sargon II in 711 BCE.
1.9: Tyre was the dominant Phoenician commercial city, famous throughout the ancient Near East as a trading hub. The treaty (berit achim, 'covenant of brotherhood') that Tyre violated by selling Israelite captives to Edom likely refers to the longstanding political alliance between Israel and Phoenicia established in the reigns of Omri, Ahab, and Solomon (1 Kgs 5:1-12; 16:31). The term 'brotherhood covenant' is diplomatic language for a formal treaty with mutual obligations; Tyre's violation of this agreement by selling Israelite captives into slavery is the specific crime indicted.
1.10: Tyre's 'wall' and 'fortresses' are its renowned defensive infrastructure — the city was built on an offshore island and was nearly impregnable (Alexander the Great required a seven-month siege in 332 BCE). The judgment against Tyre's physical fortifications anticipates the eventual Babylonian and later Greek destructions. Ezekiel 26-28 extends Amos's oracle against Tyre into a much fuller elaboration of the city's cosmic destruction.
1.11: The Edomite oracle charges Edom with 'pursuing his brother with a sword and stifling his compassion.' Edom and Israel were perpetually in conflict, but the 'brotherhood' language (achiv, his brother) activates the Jacob-Esau tradition: Edom (= Esau) and Israel (= Jacob) are twin nations, descended from brothers, who should have maintained familial solidarity. Instead, Edom's 'anger' (apo) and 'fury' (evrato) 'tear forever' — the persistent, unrelenting nature of the enmity is what makes it a transgression. The sword that Edom maintained against Israel became a defining characteristic of the Edomite-Israelite relationship (cf. Obad 10-14).
1.12: Teman and Bozrah were the two principal cities of Edom, located in the hill country south of the Dead Sea (modern Jordan). Teman was famous for its wisdom tradition (cf. Eliphaz the Temanite in Job 2:11; Jer 49:7: 'Is wisdom no more in Teman?'). Bozrah was the commercial capital and stronghold of Edom, well-attested in the archaeological record from the Iron Age II period. The fire judgment against these two centers condemns both Edom's political power (Bozrah) and its intellectual heritage (Teman).
1.13: The Ammonite oracle charges them with ripping open pregnant women in Gilead in order to enlarge their territory. This specific atrocity — attacking the Transjordanian Israelite territory of Gilead to expand Ammonite borders — is confirmed as a historical practice in 2 Kings 8:12 (Hazael's future atrocities predicted) and Hosea 13:16. The particular horror of killing pregnant women as a military strategy to eliminate future population growth is noted by later prophets and was condemned in international moral frameworks as an extreme violation. The Ammonites' goal was territorial expansion (leharchiv gevulam, to enlarge their territory).
1.14: Rabbah (modern Amman, Jordan's capital) was the primary Ammonite city, attested extensively in Iron Age II archaeology and in Egyptian and Assyrian administrative texts. The 'battle cries on the day of battle, with a storm on a day of gale' is poetic description of the chaos of military conquest — the same imagery of screaming and swirling that appears in battle accounts from ANE military records. The Ammonite king going into exile along with his officials is the standard fate of the ruling class of a conquered city.
1.15: The exile of the Ammonite king 'together with his officials' is the typical form of ANE conquest — the removal of the political and administrative elite to prevent any reconstruction of political power. Ammon's political leadership ends with the exile; the formula parallels what Amos said about Damascus (v5) and what will be said about Moab (2:3) and Israel (7:11). The phrase 'says the LORD' (amar YHWH) closes each oracle as the divine authorization for the judgment announced.

2.1: The Moabite oracle charges Moab with 'burning the bones of the king of Edom to lime' — a particularly egregious desecration of the dead. The burning of bones (saraf atzmot melek-Edom lasid) violated the ancient Near Eastern ethic that required proper burial even for enemies; the desecration of a king's remains denied him dignified existence in the afterlife under Moabite religious belief. This detail is notably a crime against Edom, not against Israel — showing that YHWH's moral jurisdiction extends beyond the covenant community to govern how nations treat each other.
2.2: Kerioth was a significant Moabite city, possibly identifiable with the modern site of Khirbet Qeiyafa or el-Qreyyat; the Mesha Stele (ca. 840 BCE), the most important Moabite inscription discovered, mentions YHWH in the context of Moabite-Israelite conflict. The 'tumult, shout, and sound of the ram's horn' (beshon beteru uvtru bequal shofar) describes the chaos of battle — the sonic landscape of military conquest in the ancient world. Moab's destruction 'in battle' is the fitting end for a nation that committed military atrocities.
2.3: The 'ruler from its midst' (shofet mikirba) and 'princes' (sarav) represent Moab's political elite — the administrative infrastructure that would need to be removed for Moab to cease functioning as an independent political entity. The removal of judges/rulers is consistent with ANE conquest strategy: without the administrative class, the nation cannot govern itself. Moab eventually became part of the Neo-Babylonian and later Persian administrative system, losing its independent political identity.
2.4: The Judah oracle is positioned just before the Israel oracle, structuring Amos's rhetorical trap: the northern kingdom audience (to whom Amos primarily speaks) would have cheered the judgment against their southern rivals as much as against the foreign nations. Judah's crime is covenantal rather than military: 'rejecting the law (Torah) of the LORD and not keeping his statutes.' This distinguishes the Judah oracle from the preceding foreign-nation oracles — Judah is judged for covenant violation, not for military atrocity. The 'false gods after which their ancestors walked' identifies the specific form of Judah's apostasy.
2.5: Jerusalem's 'fortresses' (armonot Yerushalayim) are the same term used for the foreign nations' citadels — YHWH's judgment falls on Jerusalem's defensive infrastructure with the same finality as it falls on Damascus or Tyre. This equivalence is shocking to a Judean audience: the city of David's palace-citadels are as vulnerable to YHWH's fire as the pagan capitals. The judgment of Jerusalem by fire anticipates the 586 BCE Babylonian burning, narrated in 2 Kings 25:9.
2.6: The Israel oracle, the climax of the nation-oracle series, indicts not military atrocities but economic injustice. 'Selling the righteous for silver and the needy for a pair of sandals' describes debt slavery: legally a person convicted of a debt could be sold into slavery (Lev 25:39-46), but Amos's charge is that the system was being abused — the 'righteous' (those with a legitimate case) were being sold anyway because judges were bribed. The specific mention of 'sandals' suggests trivially small debts being used as pretexts for slavery. This corresponds with the gap between rich and poor attested in 8th-century Israelite archaeological sites (large houses vs. small, ivory vs. pottery).
2.7: 'They trample the heads of the poor into the dust (hashafim al afar eretz berosh dallim)' — the idiom of 'putting one's head in the dust' is a Near Eastern expression for extreme subjugation and humiliation. 'Father and son go to the same young woman' — this is ambiguous: either the exploitation of female debt slaves (father and son sharing sexual access to a female bondservant, a violation of Lev 18:15) or the use of the same cult prostitute, defiling YHWH's holy name in either case. The phrase 'to profane my holy name' identifies this as the theological core of the indictment: social injustice is an act of blasphemy against YHWH.
2.8: 'They spread out pledged garments beside every altar' — Israelite law required that garments taken as collateral for loans be returned before nightfall (Exod 22:26-27; Deut 24:12-13), especially cloaks of the poor who would need them to sleep. Spreading these garments out beside the altar as sleeping mats or couches shows these laws were openly flouted. 'In the house of their God they drink wine obtained by fines' — the courts imposed fines that were then consumed in feasts at the sanctuary: injustice literally being eaten and drunk in YHWH's name.
2.9: YHWH's recitation of his saving acts (vv9-11) grounds the covenant obligation in historical action. The Amorites whom YHWH destroyed 'before them' were the pre-Israelite inhabitants of Canaan — described in Numbers 13:28-33 as giants (the Anakim). The Deuteronomic conquest narratives (Josh 10-12) record the defeat of Amorite kings; Amos uses the tradition to contrast YHWH's actions on Israel's behalf with Israel's ingratitude. The 'height like the cedars and strength like the oaks' is conventional hyperbole for the overwhelming power of the pre-Israelite inhabitants.
2.10: 'I also brought you up from the land of Egypt, led you through the wilderness for forty years' — the Exodus and wilderness traditions are the foundational covenant events that establish YHWH's claim on Israel's obedience. Amos appeals to this history not as a comfort but as an accusation: the same YHWH who delivered them from Egypt and led them forty years in the wilderness is now bringing judgment because they have betrayed the relationship those events established. The forty years in the wilderness is a standard reference to the entire Sinai-to-Canaan period (Num 14:33; Deut 29:5).
2.11: 'I raised up prophets from among your sons and Nazirites from among your young men' — the raising of prophets and Nazirites is presented as a divine gift to Israel parallel to the conquest and exodus: YHWH gave them not just the land but the institutions (prophetic word, Nazirite consecration) necessary for covenant life. Nazirites took vows of separation (no wine, no haircut, no corpse contact — Num 6:1-21) as signs of special dedication to YHWH; they functioned as living symbols of covenant devotion. The rhetorical question 'Is this not so, O people of Israel?' (ha'ef ken benei Yisrael) demands acknowledgment of the evidence.
2.12: 'But you gave the Nazirites wine to drink and ordered the prophets: Do not prophesy.' The twin violations — forcing Nazirites to break their vows (destroying the symbol of covenant devotion) and silencing the prophets — represent the attempt to eliminate the two primary covenant-maintenance institutions. The command 'do not prophesy' (al tinavve'u) is the attempt to mute the divine voice itself. Amos himself faced this exact command from Amaziah (7:12-13: 'Go, flee to Judah... never prophesy again at Bethel'). The silencing of the prophets is self-referential — Amos is doing the very thing Israel is commanded not to let him do.
2.13: 'Watch — I will crush you in your tracks (aneq tachat tacham), as a cart loaded with sheaves presses down.' The agricultural image of the loaded threshing-cart pressing down on its axle is vivid: Israel's accumulated sin is the weight that is about to crush them under YHWH's judgment. The word aneq (I will crush/press) is used only here in the OT, possibly a technical term for the groaning of an overloaded cart. The image shifts from the earlier fire-and-exile imagery of the nation oracles to a more agricultural metaphor suited to the Israelite peasant audience.
2.14: The five-fold impossibility of escape in vv14-16 — the swift cannot flee, the strong finds no strength, the warrior cannot save himself, the bowman cannot stand his ground, the horse cannot deliver the rider — is a comprehensive demolition of military self-confidence. The specific list corresponds to the components of an Israelite (or any ANE) army: infantry (swift and strong), archers, cavalry. No military category is exempt from YHWH's judgment. This passage addresses the same pride of military strength that Amos condemns throughout the book (cf. 6:13).
2.15: The continuation of the impossibility list: 'the bowman (tofes haqeshet) will not hold his ground; the swift-footed soldier will not escape; the horseman will not escape with his life.' The three elements — archer, light infantry, cavalry — represent the standard components of 8th-century BCE Near Eastern armies as confirmed in Assyrian reliefs and military records from the period. YHWH's judgment defeats the entire military apparatus simultaneously, leaving no category of soldier any advantage over another.
2.16: 'Even the most courageous warrior (amitz libo bagibborim; literally, the strong-hearted among the warriors) will flee away naked on that day.' The 'naked flight' (yanusu arom) of the most courageous indicates the complete dissolution of military cohesion: stripping off armor and weapons to run faster was the final act of a routed army. ANE military accounts frequently describe enemy soldiers throwing off their equipment in flight. 'On that day' links the prophecy to the Day of the LORD theology developed more fully in ch. 5.

3.1: 'Hear this word that the LORD has spoken against you, O people of Israel — against the whole family I brought up from the land of Egypt.' The summons to hear (shim'u et hadavar hazeh) is the standard prophetic call-to-attention formula. 'The whole family I brought up from the land of Egypt' — the specification that the indictment is against both halves of the divided kingdom ('the whole family', including Judah) reflects Amos's perspective that despite the political division, the covenant relationship is singular. The Exodus is again the ground for accountability.
3.2: 'You alone of all the families of the earth have I known (yadati) — therefore I will hold you accountable for all your iniquities.' The word yada (know) here means intimate covenant-knowledge, not mere factual awareness (cf. Gen 4:1; Jer 1:5). YHWH's exclusive covenant-knowledge of Israel is precisely what makes Israel's greater accountability inevitable — 'to whom much is given, much will be required' (Luke 12:48) is the underlying covenantal logic. This verse is Amos's theological foundation: special covenant status produces greater, not lesser, accountability. The Deuteronomic framework (Deut 7:6-11) assumed the same dynamic.
3.3: Amos now shifts to a series of wisdom-style rhetorical questions (vv3-8), each making the same point: every event has a cause; YHWH's prophetic word has a cause (his decision to act). 'Can two people walk together unless they have made an appointment (im lo noadau)?' — the image of two travelers who meet on the road only if they planned to meet; there are no coincidences in YHWH's dealings with Israel. The wisdom-question format connects Amos's prophetic activity to the wisdom tradition (cf. his self-identification as a 'dresser of sycamore figs', 7:14, linking him to agricultural folk-wisdom).
3.4: 'Does a lion roar in the forest when it has no prey?' — the lion-roar of v2 (YHWH roaring from Zion) is now contextualized: a lion roars only when it is about to strike. The rhetorical question implies that YHWH's prophetic declaration (the lion's roar) is evidence that the strike is imminent. Lions were common in ancient Canaan (mentioned in 1 Kgs 13:24; 2 Kgs 17:25-26; Jer 4:7; 5:6); they were well-known as both agricultural threats and royal symbols (lion hunts were a standard royal activity in Assyrian bas-reliefs). The metaphor would have immediate resonance for a rural Tekoan shepherd like Amos.
3.5: The bird-and-snare metaphors continue the cause-and-effect series: a bird falling into a snare means a trap was set; a trap springing up means a bird triggered it. Both images make the same point: when disaster strikes, it has a cause. Applied to Amos's context: the disaster coming on Israel is not random but is YHWH's deliberate judgment triggered by Israel's specific sins. The trap-and-snare imagery for divine judgment appears also in Jeremiah (5:26-27) and Psalms (69:22; 141:9), where the wicked's traps are turned against them.
3.6: 'When a ram's horn (shofar) sounds the alarm in a city, do the people not tremble? When disaster strikes a city, has not the LORD brought it about?' The shofar as a warning instrument (Num 10:5-9; Neh 4:20) and the attribution of urban catastrophe to YHWH's agency both connect to the covenant-curse framework of Deuteronomy 28 and Leviticus 26. The affirmation 'has not the LORD brought it about (im YHWH lo asah)?' is the theological claim that drives Amos's entire prophetic activity: historical disasters are YHWH's judgments, not random misfortune or the work of other gods.
3.7: 'For the Lord GOD does nothing without first disclosing his plans (sodo) to his servants the prophets.' The word sod (council, counsel, secret counsel) refers to the divine council — the heavenly assembly where YHWH makes his decisions — into which the prophet is admitted as an observer (cf. 1 Kgs 22:19-23; Jer 23:18-22; Isa 6:1-13). The prophet is YHWH's ambassador who reports to humanity what was decided in the divine council; prophecy is not private inspiration but participation in the heavenly deliberations. This verse grounds prophetic authority in access to YHWH's own counsel.
3.8: 'The lion has roared — who will not be afraid? The Lord GOD has spoken — who can withhold prophecy?' The paralleled structure equates YHWH's speech with the lion's roar (as in v4). The compulsion of prophecy — the inability to withhold it when YHWH has spoken — is the inner experience of the prophetic vocation (cf. Jer 20:9: 'there is something like a burning fire shut up in my bones... I am weary of holding it in, and I cannot'). Amos's defense of his prophetic activity (7:14-15: 'the LORD took me from following the flock and said to me, Go, prophesy') reflects exactly this compulsion.
3.9: 'Proclaim to the citadels of Ashdod and to the strongholds in the land of Egypt: Gather on the mountains of Samaria.' The irony of summoning the pagan nations (Philistia and Egypt) to witness Israel's moral disorder is biting: the nations renowned for their own injustice (Ashdod was condemned in ch1) are invited to observe that Israel's internal social conditions are worse. The 'mountains of Samaria' (Har Shomron) were the elevated terrain around the capital, where the wealthy built their second homes (cf. 4:1: cows of Bashan on Mount Samaria). The foreign nations are cast as witnesses at Israel's trial.
3.10: 'They do not know how to act with integrity (lo yad'u asot nachona), declares the LORD — those who hoard violence and plunder in their fortresses.' The inability to 'do right' is not merely moral failure but a kind of moral blindness — they do not know, cannot perceive, what right action looks like. The accumulation of 'violence and plunder' (chamas veshed) in the fortress-citadels is the physical evidence of structural injustice: the wealth stored in the elite residences came from the exploitation documented in 2:6-8.
3.11: 'Therefore this is what the Lord GOD says: An enemy will surround the land; he will strip away your defense and plunder your fortresses.' The 'enemy' (tzar) is left unnamed — Amos does not yet name Assyria explicitly in this oracle, though it is the obvious referent given the political situation. The stripping away of defenses and plundering of fortresses directly reverses the accumulation condemned in v10: the fortresses filled with plunder will themselves be plundered. This reversal-of-plunder justice is characteristic of the covenant-curse framework (Lev 26:33; Deut 28:33).
3.12: 'As the shepherd snatches from the lion's mouth two legs or a scrap of an ear, so will the Israelites living in Samaria be rescued — with the corner of a bed and a scrap of couch.' The shepherd saving two legs or an ear from a lion-kill was a legal procedure for proving that the animal had died rather than been stolen (Exod 22:13); the fragments served as evidence. Amos's ironic use of this image: the 'rescue' of Israel will be as pitiful as the fragments that prove something was consumed — not a real salvation but the evidence that something died. The 'corner of a bed and a scrap of couch' specifically mocks the ivory-inlaid luxury furniture condemned in 6:4.
3.13: 'Hear this and testify against the house of Jacob, declares the Lord GOD, the God of armies.' The divine title Elohim Tzvaot (God of armies/hosts) appears in Amos four times; it emphasizes the military sovereignty of YHWH over all human armies — the same 'armies' that Israel's wealthy class trusted in their fortresses are under YHWH's command. The summons to testify (ve'ha'idu) continues the juridical language that runs through ch. 3: Israel is on trial, and the nations (v9) and now an unnamed witness are called to testify against Jacob.
3.14: 'On the day I call Israel to account (paqdi al pish'ei Yisrael), I will also strike down the altars of Bethel; the horns of the altar will be cut off and fall to the ground.' The altar-horns at Bethel are the specific cultic infrastructure at the northern kingdom's primary sanctuary. The horns of the altar were the projection of divine protection — grasping the altar-horns was the classic act of seeking asylum (1 Kgs 1:50; 2:28). YHWH striking the horns off eliminates both the cult and the asylum-function simultaneously: there is no refuge in the altar of a corrupt cult.
3.15: 'I will demolish both the winter house and the summer house; the houses adorned with ivory will perish and the great houses will be swept away.' The archaeological record from 8th-century Samaria confirms both the luxury described here and its eventual destruction. The Samaria Ivories — over 500 ivory fragments found at the excavation of ancient Samaria — were plaques and decorative panels from elite furniture exactly like that described in Amos 3:15 and 6:4 (beds and couches inlaid with ivory). The 'winter house and summer house' are the seasonal residences of the wealthy, confirmed in both Iron Age archaeology and Egyptian and Assyrian administrative texts.

4.1: 'Hear this word, you cows of Bashan on Mount Samaria.' The 'cows of Bashan' was a striking insult: Bashan (modern Golan Heights) was famous for its fat, well-fed cattle (Ps 22:12; Ezek 39:18; Deut 32:14). Amos applies this image to the wealthy women of Samaria who 'oppress the poor and crush the needy and say to their husbands, Bring us wine to drink.' The social analysis is specific: the wealthy women's demand for wine drives their husbands to economic exploitation. This connection of elite female consumption to systemic poverty reflects the archaeological evidence of Samarian wealth stratification in the 8th century BCE.
4.2: 'The Lord GOD has sworn by his holiness' — the divine oath by YHWH's own holiness is the highest possible guarantor; YHWH cannot swear by anything greater than himself (cf. Heb 6:13). The image of being dragged away 'with fishhooks' (betzinot, possibly fishhooks or thorns) and 'on fishhooks' (besirot dugah) is disputed in translation but clearly describes captive deportation — perhaps with nose-rings or similar instruments used in ANE deportation records (Assyrian reliefs show captives led by ropes attached to nose/lip rings). The Assyrian deportation of the northern kingdom's population in 722-720 BCE would fulfill this threat.
4.3: 'You will be dragged through the breaches in the walls, each woman straight out before her, and flung into Harmon.' 'Harmon' (haHarmonah) is obscure — possibly a place name, a word for the dung heap, or a geographical term for the Hermon range north of Israel. The image of women being pushed through the city's breached walls during conquest is consistent with ANE military descriptions of siege and conquest. Samaria's walls were eventually breached by Sargon II's Assyrian army in 722/720 BCE after a three-year siege (2 Kgs 17:5-6).
4.4: 'Come to Bethel and rebel; go to Gilgal and multiply your rebellion; bring your sacrifices every morning, your tithes every three years.' The bitter sarcasm of the invitation to continue the cult at Israel's two major sanctuaries (Bethel and Gilgal were both associated with Jacob-traditions and Israelite history — Gen 12:8; Josh 4:19; 1 Sam 11:15) is characteristic of Amos's rhetorical style. 'Rebel' (pish'u) is the same word used throughout the nation-oracle formula ('for three crimes'); the cult itself, Amos says, is the site of rebellion. The specific acts mentioned — daily sacrifices, triennial tithes — were the required cultic obligations, but performed in the context of social injustice they become rebellion.
4.5: 'Offer your leavened thanksgiving sacrifices and proclaim your freewill offerings, announce them publicly — for so you love to do, O people of Israel, declares the Lord GOD.' The command to publicly announce freewill offerings is sarcasm targeting the public display of piety: those who 'love' to announce their generosity are performing religious theater rather than worshipping YHWH. Leavened bread was normally prohibited in sacrifices (Lev 2:11) except in certain thanksgiving contexts (Lev 7:13); the specific mention may be pointing to liturgical irregularities at Bethel. The phrase 'for so you love to do' drips with contempt for performative religion.
4.6: 'I gave you empty mouths in all your cities and shortage of food in all your towns — yet you did not return to me (velo shavtem adai), declares the LORD.' The first of five covenant-discipline episodes (vv6-11), each ending with the refrain 'yet you did not return to me,' forms a theological argument: YHWH used calibrated hardship (famine, drought, blight, plague, partial destruction) as covenant correctives before bringing total judgment. The 'empty mouths' (niqayon shinayim, literally 'cleanness/emptiness of teeth') is a vivid idiom for famine: teeth with nothing to chew on. The refrain connects to Hosea's theology (Hos 6:1: 'come, let us return to the LORD').
4.7: 'I also withheld the rain from you three months before harvest; I sent rain on one city but not on another city; one field would receive rain while the field that received no rain would dry up.' The selective pattern of rain and drought — one city receiving rain while another goes dry, one field watered while its neighbor bakes — is a remarkable description of the micro-climatic agricultural conditions of the Levant, where hills and valleys can have dramatically different rainfall. This was understood as YHWH's control over the weather (cf. Deut 28:23-24: 'the sky over your head will be bronze, the ground beneath your feet iron'). Archaeological grain-storage studies confirm periodic drought crises in Iron Age II Palestine.
4.8: 'Two or three cities staggered to another city for water and could not get enough — yet you did not return to me.' The image of urban populations physically migrating to seek water is consistent with the water-dependence of ancient Levantine cities on springs, cisterns, and seasonal rainfall. Samaria's water supply was partly dependent on the Wadi Farah and other seasonal sources; in drought years, the economic and human cost would be severe. The 'staggering' (nau, wandered/staggered) reflects physical exhaustion alongside the drought's economic impact.
4.9: 'I struck your crops with blight and mildew; locusts devoured your many gardens and vineyards, your fig trees and olive trees.' Agricultural blight (shidafon, scorching wind) and mildew (yraqon, yellowing/mildew) are paired in the covenant-curse formulas of Deuteronomy 28:22 and in Solomon's temple prayer (1 Kgs 8:37). Locust plagues were catastrophic and well-documented throughout ancient Near Eastern records; the Joel 1-2 locust description provides the most detailed biblical account. The specific mention of gardens, vineyards, fig trees, and olive trees — the four main crops of ancient Israelite agriculture — shows that the entire agricultural system was struck.
4.10: 'I sent among you a plague like those of Egypt (bedeqer Mitzraim); I killed your young men with the sword, took your horses away, and made the stench of your camp go up into your nostrils.' The 'plague like those of Egypt' deliberately invokes the Exodus narrative in reverse: the same plagues YHWH used to liberate Israel from Egypt are now turned against Israel in its own land. The sword deaths and capture of horses suggest specific military encounters — probably border raids by Arameans or other opponents during periods of weakness. The 'stench of the camp' after a military defeat is the putrefaction of unburied bodies.
4.11: 'I overthrew some of you as God overthrew Sodom and Gomorrah; you were like a stick snatched from the fire.' The Sodom-and-Gomorrah comparison is the most extreme point on the covenant-curse spectrum — the paradigmatic destruction that YHWH uses as the standard for ultimate judgment (Gen 19:24-25; Deut 29:23; Isa 1:9; Jer 49:18). The 'stick snatched from the fire' (ud mutzal misreifah) is a remnant-after-disaster image: some survived but only as barely-saved debris. This phrase recurs in Zechariah 3:2 for Joshua the high priest's restoration after exile. Yet even this extreme near-destruction 'did not cause you to return' — the refrain's final occurrence before the threat shifts to total judgment.
4.12: 'Therefore, because I will do this to you, O Israel, prepare yourself to meet your God.' The 'this' is deliberately vague — after the catalogue of graduated covenant disciplines, the next judgment is something beyond the list, implied to be worse than Sodom's destruction. 'Prepare to meet your God' (hikkon liqrat Eloheicha Yisrael) is the most terrifying summons in Amos — framed like a theophanic preparation (Exod 19:11: 'be ready for the third day, for on the third day the LORD will come down'), but here directed toward judgment rather than covenant. The verse refuses to name the judgment, forcing the listener's imagination to supply it.
4.13: 'For this is he who forms the mountains and creates the wind, who reveals to humanity what he is thinking, who turns dawn into darkness and treads on the heights of the earth — the LORD God Almighty is his name.' The doxology closes ch. 4 with a hymn to YHWH's creative sovereignty. Three creation acts (forming mountains, creating wind, turning dawn to darkness) and one relational act (revealing his thoughts to humanity through prophecy) establish that the God who is about to judge Israel is the Creator. The triple divine title at the close — 'the LORD God Almighty' (YHWH Elohei Tzvaot shemo) — is the fullest possible expression of divine authority in Amos's vocabulary.

5.1: 'Hear this word that I take up over you in lamentation, O house of Israel.' The shift from accusation (rib, lawsuit) to lamentation (qinah) is a literary signal: Amos now adopts the funeral-lament meter (the distinctive 3+2 stress pattern of Hebrew funeral poetry) to sing Israel's dirge before it dies. This is the form in which ancient Near Eastern professional mourners lamented the dead — Amos performs the funeral as a prophetic act before the event, which is more terrifying than the accusation because it presupposes the verdict is already final.
5.2: 'Fallen, no more to rise, is the virgin Israel; forsaken on her land, with no one to raise her up.' The 'virgin Israel' (betulat Yisrael) — the nation personified as a young woman who has not yet been married/destroyed — is portrayed as already fallen in the prophetic vision. The lament is in past tense in the Hebrew, a prophetic perfect expressing certainty: it is as though it has already happened. The specific phrase 'no one to raise her up' (ein meqimah) refers to the absence of a kinsman-redeemer who would restore an Israelite woman to social standing — YHWH is not performing that role here because the judgment has been determined.
5.3: 'For thus says the Lord GOD: The city that marches out a thousand will have a hundred left, and the one that marches out a hundred will have ten left to the house of Israel.' The decimation-by-nine-tenths is a catastrophic attrition rate — only 10% survival from military engagement. This is not a metaphor for partial defeat but for virtually total annihilation. The specific numbers (1000 → 100; 100 → 10) follow the Deuteronomic curse pattern of progressive reduction (Deut 28:62: 'you will be left few in number, whereas you were as numerous as the stars in the sky').
5.4: 'For thus says the LORD to the house of Israel: Seek me and live (dershuni vichyu).' After the funeral lament, YHWH's invitation to life is startling and merciful: the judgment is not yet sealed for those who will turn. The word darash (seek) is the covenant-seeking term that appears throughout the prophets (Deut 4:29; Jer 29:13; Hos 10:12); it describes the active, intentional orientation toward YHWH that is the response to the prophetic call. 'Seek me and live' is structurally similar to John 5:24 and 10:10 — finding YHWH means life; finding his cultic substitutes means death (v5).
5.5: 'Do not seek Bethel, do not go to Gilgal, do not journey to Beersheba.' The three pilgrim sanctuaries — Bethel (Jacob's stairway, Gen 28:10-22), Gilgal (the first camp after crossing the Jordan, Josh 4:19-24), and Beersheba (Abraham's covenant with Abimelech, Gen 21:31) — are all associated with foundational covenant events. YHWH's command not to seek him at these places is the prophetic claim that the cult at these sites has become an obstacle to finding YHWH rather than a means of access. 'Gilgal will surely go into exile, and Bethel will come to nothing' — the plays on words (Hagilgal galoh yigleh; Beit-el yihyeh le'aven) make Gilgal a wordplay on 'go into exile' and Bethel into 'Beth-aven' (House of Wickedness, cf. Hos 4:15).
5.6: 'Seek the LORD and live, or he will sweep through the house of Joseph like a fire, and it will devour, with no one to quench it, for Bethel.' The 'house of Joseph' — Ephraim and Manasseh, the two dominant northern tribes — stands as a synecdoche for the entire northern kingdom. The fire that 'devours Bethel' (the main northern sanctuary) with no one to quench it is the covenant-curse fire of total judgment (cf. 1:4-2:5 where YHWH sends fire on every condemned nation). The link between seeking YHWH and avoiding the fire is direct: the sanctuary they worship at will be burned; only YHWH himself — not the cult site — can provide protection.
5.7: 'You who turn justice into wormwood and hurl righteousness to the ground!' Wormwood (la'anah) is a bitter, toxic plant (Artemisia absinthium) used throughout the OT as an image of bitter judgment (Jer 9:15; 23:15; Lam 3:15,19; Rev 8:11). The perversion of justice into something toxic and the casting of righteousness to the ground is a spatial reversal of how justice should work: justice should be raised up and celebrated; instead it is thrown down and poisoned. This indictment (v7) forms an inclusio with the justice-exhortation (v24: let justice roll down like waters) around the central doxology (vv8-9).
5.8: 'He who made the Pleiades and Orion, who turns midnight into dawn and darkens the day into night' — the second of Amos's doxologies (cf. 4:13; 9:5-6), celebrating YHWH's cosmic power over the created order. The Pleiades (Kimah) and Orion (Kesil) were well-known star clusters in the ancient Near East; in Akkadian astronomical texts, Orion was associated with the god of chaos. YHWH's control over these constellations asserts his sovereignty over the forces that other cultures considered independent deities. The alternation of dawn and darkness reflects YHWH's sovereignty over the day/night cycle — the same darkness Amos uses for the Day of the LORD imagery (5:18-20).
5.9: 'He flashes destruction on the stronghold and brings ruin upon the fortress.' The same YHWH who created the Pleiades and Orion also destroys fortifications — the cosmic Creator is also the destroyer of human military infrastructure. This direct connection between creation theology and judgment theology is characteristic of 8th-century prophecy (cf. Isa 40:12-26 where creation sovereignty underpins YHWH's ability to judge nations). The fortress/stronghold language (meteg/mibtsar) echoes the fortress vocabulary of the nation-oracle series (chs. 1-2), tying the doxology to the judgment framework.
5.10: 'They hate the one who speaks up in court (mochiach bashaer) and despise the one who tells the truth.' The gate (sha'ar) was the site of the ancient Israelite legal system — elders sat at the gate to adjudicate disputes (Ruth 4:1-12; Deut 16:18). The person who 'reproves in the gate' is both the elder who gives legal verdicts and the prophet who speaks truth publicly. The hatred of those who speak honestly in legal proceedings reflects the corruption of the justice system documented elsewhere in Amos (2:6-8; 8:4-6). The connection between the prophetic role and the legal role is significant: Amos is doing in public what the corrupt judges refuse to do at the gate.
5.11: 'Therefore because you trample on the poor and take from them exactions of grain, you have built houses of dressed stone, but you will not live in them; you have planted pleasant vineyards, but you will not drink their wine.' The covenant curse of Deuteronomy 28:30-31 ('you will build a house, but you will not live in it; you will plant a vineyard, but you will not eat its fruit') is applied with specific precision to the economic exploitation Amos has described. The 'houses of dressed stone (batei gazit)' are the ashlar-construction luxury residences confirmed in Samaria's archaeological record — more expensive and impressive than the standard rubble construction of ordinary houses.
5.12: 'For I know how many are your crimes and how great are your sins — you who afflict the righteous, take bribes, and turn aside the needy in court.' YHWH's direct knowledge of the specific offenses (ki yadati rabbim pish'eichem) removes any pretense of legal process: YHWH himself is both witness and judge. The three specific offenses — afflicting the righteous (tzadikim), taking bribes (kofer), and deflecting the needy at the gate — are the classic forms of juridical corruption in the ancient Israelite legal system, each explicitly prohibited in the Mosaic law (Exod 23:2-8; Deut 16:19).
5.13: 'Therefore the prudent will keep silent in such a time, for it is an evil time.' The 'prudent person' (maskil) who keeps silent is not indifferent but tactically quiet — in a context where the powerful control the courts (vv10-12), the wise person knows that public speech is futile and dangerous. The 'evil time' (ki et ra'ah hi) is a diagnosis of the moment's moral condition: it is not merely dangerous to speak but structurally evil. This verse may be a wry self-commentary by Amos on the situation of a prophet who is commanded to speak in exactly such an evil time.
5.14: 'Seek good and not evil, so that you may live; and so the LORD, the God Almighty, will be with you, just as you say.' The phrase 'just as you say' (ka'asher amartem) is the sharp irony: Israel claims YHWH is with them (cf. Micah 3:11: 'Is not the LORD among us? Disaster will not come upon us'), but the claim is empty unless it is grounded in the seeking of good and avoidance of evil. The conditional form of the promise ('so that... will be with you') makes clear that the divine presence Israel presumes is actually contingent on the moral orientation Amos demands.
5.15: 'Hate evil, love good, and establish justice in the gate; it may be that the LORD, the God Almighty, will be gracious to the remnant of Joseph.' The 'remnant of Joseph' (she'erit Yosef) is the first hint in Amos of a possible survival — a remnant preserved through the judgment. The 'may be' (ulai) is characteristically prophetic hedging: the possibility of grace, not its guarantee. 'Establishing justice in the gate' is the structural demand — reforming the legal institutions that Amos has indicted is the concrete expression of 'seeking good.'
5.16: 'Therefore this is what the LORD, the God Almighty, says: There will be wailing in all the public squares and cries of anguish in all the streets; farmers will be called to mourn and professional mourners to wail.' The professional mourners (mesofedim) and 'those skilled in lamentation' (yode'ei nehi) reflect the well-developed mourning industry of the ancient Near East, where funeral rituals were elaborate public events requiring skilled performers. ANE mourning texts (from Mesopotamia and Egypt) document the range of mourning practices — wailing, tearing garments, shaving heads — that Amos references. The invocation of the farmers to mourn is the inversion of the harvest festival: instead of joy at the harvest, there will be mourning at the loss of everything.
5.17: 'In all the vineyards there will be wailing, for I will pass through your midst, says the LORD.' 'I will pass through (avarti bekirbecha)' echoes the Passover narrative: the same formula used in Exodus 12:12 (YHWH passing through Egypt to strike the firstborn) is now directed at Israel. The reversal is total: the Passover was the event that demonstrated YHWH's judgment was against Egypt and protection was for Israel; now YHWH's 'passing through' brings judgment to Israel as it once did to Egypt.
5.18: 'Woe to you who desire the day of the LORD! Why do you want the day of the LORD? It is darkness, not light.' The 'Day of the LORD' (yom YHWH) had developed in Israel's theology as the expected day of divine intervention on Israel's behalf — a day of YHWH's victory over Israel's enemies. Amos's reversal is devastating: those who long for the Day of the LORD as the day of national triumph are longing for their own judgment. The concept of the Day of the LORD likely originated in the tradition of YHWH as divine warrior (cf. the 'day of Midian', Isa 9:4; the 'day of battle', Zech 14:3), representing YHWH's decisive defeat of his enemies.
5.19: 'It will be as though someone fled from a lion only to meet a bear, or went home and rested his hand on the wall only to have a snake bite him.' The triple impossibility of escape — from lion, from bear, from the house itself — mirrors the impossibility-of-flight list in 2:14-16. The specific animals (lion, bear, snake) are all dangers present in ancient Canaan's agricultural and pastoral landscape; the progression from outdoor danger (lion in the open) to outdoor danger (bear nearby) to indoor danger (snake in the house itself) closes every possible place of refuge. There is nowhere the Day of the LORD does not reach.
5.20: 'Is not the day of the LORD darkness, not light — pitch-dark, without a ray of brightness?' The reiteration of the darkness imagery (v18 repeated) closes the Day of the LORD section with emphatic finality: there is no exception, no partial brightness, no 'ray of brightness' (nogah lo bo, no shining in it). The absolute character of the darkness — not merely dim but 'pitch-dark' (afelah velo nogah) — corresponds to the completeness of the judgment that Amos has been describing throughout the book.
5.21: 'I hate, I despise your feasts, and I take no delight in your solemn assemblies (azaroteichem).' The solemn assemblies (atzeret/azarah) were the closing festivals of the major pilgrimage feasts (Lev 23:36; Deut 16:8). YHWH's rejection of the entire cultic calendar — 'I hate... I despise (sane'ti mas'ati)' — uses the strongest possible language of divine rejection. This is not a moderate criticism of cultic abuse but a comprehensive repudiation of the cult as it is actually being performed. The same dynamic appears in Isaiah 1:11-15 and Jeremiah 7:21-23.
5.22: 'Even though you offer me your burnt offerings and grain offerings, I will not accept them; and the offerings of well-being of your fatted animals I will not look upon.' The three major sacrifice categories — burnt offerings (olah), grain offerings (minchah), and well-being offerings (shlemim) — are explicitly rejected. These were the three primary forms of Israelite sacrifice (Lev 1-3); their rejection is the rejection of the entire Levitical sacrificial system as it is actually being practiced at Bethel and Gilgal. YHWH 'will not look upon (lo abit)' the fatted animals — refusing to acknowledge even the costliest offerings.
5.23: 'Take away from me the noise of your songs; I will not listen to the melody of your harps.' The musical component of Israelite worship — the Levitical singing and instrumental accompaniment that was developed under David and maintained in the Temple tradition (1 Chr 25:1-7; Ps 150) — is also rejected. The 'noise' (hamon) of songs and the 'melody' (zimrat nevalekha) of harps are the cultic music that accompanied the sacrifices. YHWH's rejection of both sacrifice and music is the rejection of the entire multi-sensory experience of Israelite worship. The positive counterpart comes in v24.
5.24: 'But let justice roll down like waters, and righteousness like an ever-flowing stream (venagar kamayim mishpat utzdaqah kenahal eitan).' The most famous verse in Amos — and one of the most famous in all prophetic literature — posits the alternative to the rejected cult. The water metaphors are not merely aesthetic: in the semi-arid Levant, a 'perennial stream' (nahal eitan, a wadi that flows year-round rather than seasonally) was precious and rare. Most wadis were seasonal, drying up in summer. YHWH wants justice that flows like a perennial spring — constant, reliable, not seasonal or occasional. This verse was quoted by Martin Luther King Jr. in the 'I Have a Dream' speech (1963), the most famous modern appropriation of any OT prophetic text.
5.25: 'Did you bring me sacrifices and offerings forty years in the wilderness, O house of Israel?' The rhetorical question revisits the wilderness tradition with a challenging subtext: the sacrificial cult as it was practiced at Bethel was not the original form of Israel's relationship with YHWH. The wilderness period — when there was no elaborate temple cult — was nonetheless the period of most intimate YHWH-Israel relationship (Jer 2:2-3: 'I remember the devotion of your youth... you followed me in the wilderness'). The question implies that the cult at Bethel is a historical accretion that has displaced the covenantal relationship it was supposed to express.
5.26: 'You carried Sikkuth your king and Kiyyun your star-god, your images that you made for yourselves.' This verse contains one of the most textually difficult passages in Amos; 'Sikkuth' and 'Kiyyun' are probably Assyrian astral deity names (possibly Sakkuth/Saturn and Kewanu/Saturn's star in the Akkadian tradition), with the vowels replaced by the Hebrew vowels for shiqquts (detestable thing) — a standard scribal technique for expressing contempt for foreign gods. The reference to astral worship reflects the Assyrian cultural influence on northern Israel during this period, when Assyrian religious iconography spread through trade and political contact.
5.27: 'Therefore I will take you into exile beyond Damascus, says the LORD, whose name is the God Almighty.' The closure of the first major section of Amos with the threat of exile 'beyond Damascus' anticipates the Assyrian deportation of 722-720 BCE, when the northern kingdom's population was deported to various locations in the Assyrian Empire (2 Kgs 17:6: Halah, Habor, the river of Gozan, and the cities of the Medes — all well beyond Damascus to the northeast). The specification 'beyond Damascus' may reflect Amos's knowledge of the Assyrian deportation routes, which went through Damascus to Mesopotamia. The divine title closing the chapter — 'whose name is the God Almighty' — frames the judgment with YHWH's sovereign authority over all the armies that will enact it.

6.1: 'Woe to those who are at ease in Zion, and to those who feel secure on the mountain of Samaria, the notable men of the first of the nations, to whom the house of Israel comes!' The oracle opens with the dual address to Jerusalem (Zion) and Samaria — both kingdoms simultaneously indicted. The 'notable men of the first of the nations' (nequvei reshit hagoyim) reflects the self-understanding of the Israelite elite during Jeroboam II's reign: Israel had extended its borders from Lebo-hamath to the Sea of the Arabah (2 Kings 14:25), and the ruling class believed this territorial success confirmed their status. The phrase 'to whom the house of Israel comes' — clients coming to patronize the powerful — captures the court culture of the period.
6.2: 'Pass over to Calneh, and see, and from there go to Hamath the great; then go down to Gath of the Philistines. Are you better than these kingdoms? Or is their territory greater than your territory?' Three cities cited as object lessons: Calneh (Kullania in north Syria, conquered by Tiglath-Pileser III c. 738 BCE and likely already reduced when Amos spoke), Hamath the Great (the Syrian capital on the Orontes), and Gath of the Philistines (traditionally one of the five Philistine cities, destroyed by Uzziah according to 2 Chr 26:6). Whether these were conquered before Amos spoke (making them warnings) or after (a later gloss is possible) is debated. The rhetorical point is: are you more secure than they were?
6.3: 'O you who put far away the day of disaster and bring near the seat of violence!' The syntax is inverted for emphasis: the elite who distance themselves psychologically from any thought of judgment ('the day of disaster is far off') are simultaneously 'bringing near the seat of violence' — their own unjust structures are creating the conditions for the judgment they deny. The 'seat of violence' (shevet chamas) means both a session/throne of violence (institutionalized oppression) and a plague/rod of violence (its punishment).
6.4: 'Woe to those who lie on beds of ivory and stretch themselves out on their couches, and eat lambs from the flock and calves from the midst of the stall.' The 'beds of ivory' (mitot shen) are archaeologically confirmed: excavations at Samaria (conducted by Harvard in 1908-10 and Cambridge/British Institutions in 1931-35) uncovered hundreds of ivory plaques and inlays from 9th-8th century BCE contexts — furniture decoration with Phoenician and Egyptian motifs. Ahab's 'ivory house' is mentioned in 1 Kings 22:39. The lambs from the flock and stall-fed calves represent elite consumption: animals specifically fattened for banquets rather than ordinary flock animals.
6.5: 'Who sing idle songs to the sound of the harp and like David improvise on musical instruments.' 'Like David' (keDavid) — the comparison is pointed: David played the harp as an act of worship (1 Sam 16:23, the soothing of Saul; 2 Sam 6:5, dancing before the ark; Ps titles). The elite of Samaria use David's instrument for self-entertainment. 'Improvise' (parat, literally: spread out, elaborate) suggests sophisticated musical composition — the artistic energy that should go to worship goes to private pleasure. The contrast between David's purposeful music and their idle songs indicts the misuse of cultural gifts.
6.6: 'Who drink wine in bowls and anoint themselves with the finest oils, but are not grieved over the ruin of Joseph!' 'Wine in bowls' (yishtu bemizrekey yayin) — mizrak is the large cultic bowl used in temple ritual (Exod 27:3, Num 4:14); drinking wine in ceremonial bowls represents either shocking excess (using sacred vessels for parties) or simply oversized vessels. The anointing with 'the finest oils' (shemen roshim) is luxury personal care. The climax: 'not grieved over the ruin of Joseph' (velo nechelu al-shever Yoseph) — the northern kingdom was sometimes called 'Joseph' after the patriarch whose sons Ephraim and Manasseh constituted most of the northern territory.
6.7: 'Therefore they shall now be the first of those who go into exile, and the revelry of those who stretch themselves out shall pass away.' The reversal: the 'first of the nations' (v1) become the first into exile. The verb 'go captive' (yiglu) was fulfilled literally: 2 Kings 17:6 records that Sargon II of Assyria carried the Israelites away to Halah, Habor, the river of Gozan, and the cities of the Medes. The 'revelry of those who stretch themselves out' (mirzach seruchim) — the marzeah was a known institution in the ancient Levant: a wealthy club or guild that held regular banquets, associated with both Baal worship and ancestral cults.
6.8: 'The Lord GOD has sworn by himself, declares the LORD, the God of hosts: I abhor the pride of Jacob and hate his strongholds, and I will deliver up the city and all that is in it.' YHWH swearing by himself (nishba adoaniy YHWH benafshoa) — since there is no higher authority to invoke, God swears by his own being (cf. Gen 22:16, Heb 6:13-14). In the ancient world, oath-swearing by a deity's life was the strongest possible pledge. 'The pride of Jacob' (ge'on Ya'aqov) — the same phrase used in 8:7 as a divine title ('the LORD has sworn by the pride of Jacob') — here YHWH abhors what he is elsewhere. The distinction: divine pride is glory; human pride in Samaria is self-deception about their security.
6.9: 'And if ten men remain in one house, they shall die.' The scene shifts abruptly to a future aftermath: pestilence (deber) so severe that a house with ten survivors still ends in death. The specific number ten suggests a family unit that survived the initial military disaster only to succumb to disease. Ancient Near Eastern warfare typically resulted in three waves of death: the battle itself, the subsequent famine as agriculture collapsed, and the plague that followed displacement and crowded conditions — exactly the pattern of Deut 28:21-22.
6.10: 'And when one's relative, the one who anoints him for burial, shall take him up to bring the bones out of the house, and shall say to him who is in the innermost parts of the house, Is there still anyone with you? he shall say, No. Then he shall say, Silence! We must not mention the name of the LORD.' The specific cultural practice: a kinsman comes to retrieve bones for proper burial (secondary burial of bones was practiced in ancient Israel, especially in clay ossuaries from the Iron Age onward). 'We must not mention the name of the LORD' (ki lo lehazkkir beshem YHWH) — in the context of divine judgment, invoking YHWH's name might intensify the danger. This reflects a form of the OT teaching that YHWH's name is not to be invoked carelessly.
6.11: 'For behold, the LORD commands, and the great house shall be struck down into fragments, and the little house into bits.' The 'great house' (habayit hagadol) and 'little house' (habayit haqatan) — both destroyed, with the distinction that the great house is struck into fragments (rezazim, cracked pieces) and the little house into bits (baqiim, splinters). The differentiation may reflect that the wealthy stone houses (like those found in the Samaria excavations, with their fine ashlar masonry) require more force to destroy than the ordinary mudbrick houses, but both will be leveled equally.
6.12: 'Do horses run on rocks? Does one plow the sea with oxen? But you have turned justice into poison and the fruit of righteousness into wormwood.' The rhetorical questions establish natural impossibility: horses do not run on bare rock (they would break their legs); oxen do not plow the sea. Yet Israel has accomplished the morally equivalent impossibility: turning mishpat (justice, the system of covenant law) into poison (rosh, a bitter/poisonous plant, also gall) and the 'fruit of righteousness' (peri tzedaqah) into wormwood (la'anah). The covenant legal system designed to produce justice has been inverted into a mechanism of oppression.
6.13: 'You who rejoice in Lo-debar, who say, Have we not by our own strength taken Karnaim for ourselves?' Lo-debar (lo-davar) literally means 'nothing/no-thing' — the place name is a pun. Karnaim means 'two horns' (the symbol of strength). Whether these are actual military victories of Jeroboam II's campaigns (cf. 2 Kings 14:25) or symbolic names is debated; most likely they were real towns in Transjordan captured during Israel's territorial expansion, but Amos uses the place names for their punning value: they rejoice in 'Nothing' and celebrate 'Horns' (military power) as if it were their own achievement.
6.14: 'For behold, I will raise up against you a nation, O house of Israel, declares the LORD, the God of hosts; and they shall oppress you from Lebo-hamath to the Brook of the Arabah.' The 'nation' is Assyria — unnamed but identifiable in context. Lebo-hamath (the northern ideal border of Israel, Num 34:8, Ezek 47:15) to the Brook of the Arabah (the southern border, possibly the Wadi el-Hasa), representing the full territorial extent of Israel under Jeroboam II (2 Kings 14:25). The same territory that represented Israel's peak expansion will be the territory of Assyrian oppression — the blessing of territory becomes the curse of occupation.

7.1: 'This is what the Lord GOD showed me: behold, he was forming locusts when the latter growth was just beginning to sprout, and behold, it was the latter growth after the king's mowings.' The first of five visions (ch7:1-3, 7:4-6, 7:7-9, 8:1-3, 9:1). 'The latter growth after the king's mowings' (hineh leqesh achar gizze hamelech) refers to the agricultural practice: the first mowing of the spring crop went to the king as royal taxation (compare 1 Sam 8:15: 'he will take one-tenth of your grain'). The 'latter growth' (leqesh) was the smaller second crop that the farmer kept. YHWH forming locusts (goze arbeh) to destroy this second crop — the farmer's share after the king took his — would mean total ruin for ordinary people.
7.2: 'When the locusts had finished eating the vegetation of the land, I said, O Lord GOD, please forgive! How can Jacob stand? He is so small!' Amos intercedes — perhaps unexpectedly for a prophet of judgment. 'How can Jacob stand? He is so small' (mi yaqum Ya'aqov ki qaton hu) — the argument from weakness, not from merit. This is similar to the Mosaic intercession pattern (Exod 32:11-14, Num 14:13-19) where the prophet argues not for Israel's righteousness but for divine mercy and YHWH's own purpose. 'Jacob' as the name for the nation — the patriarch who himself was small and dependent on divine provision.
7.3: 'The LORD relented concerning this: It shall not be, said the LORD.' YHWH 'repented' (nacham) of this judgment — the same verb used in Gen 6:6-7 (YHWH regretting making humanity) and Exod 32:14 (YHWH relenting from destroying Israel after the golden calf). The theological paradox of prophetic intercession: the predetermined judgment is modified by prophetic prayer. The first two visions both end with divine relenting in response to intercession — establishing that Amos's intercession was effective. The third vision (7:7-9) notably does not include intercession and does not end with relenting.
7.4: 'This is what the Lord GOD showed me: behold, the Lord GOD was calling for a judgment by fire, and it devoured the great deep and was eating up the land.' The second vision: fire of judgment devouring the 'great deep' (tehom rabbah) — the subterranean water source that feeds springs and wells (cf. Gen 7:11, 49:25). A fire that consumes the deep would dry up all underground water, creating comprehensive drought. The vision escalates from locusts (destroying the harvest) to fire (destroying the water supply itself), building the case that YHWH has multiple instruments of comprehensive judgment available.
7.5: 'Then I said, O Lord GOD, please cease! How can Jacob stand? He is so small!' The second intercession uses the same form as the first — the repetition is liturgically deliberate, like a pattern of prayer. The parallel structure (vision → divine action → prophetic intercession → divine relenting) establishes Amos's intercessory role. Unlike the writing prophets who are often presented primarily as proclaimers of judgment, Amos here shows the intercessory dimension — standing between YHWH and the people to plead, as Moses did (Exod 32:11, Deut 9:18-20).
7.6: 'The LORD relented concerning this: This also shall not be, said the Lord GOD.' The second relenting follows the same pattern. 'This also shall not be' — the emphatic 'also' marks the continuation of the intercessory success. But the three-part structure (locusts, fire, plumb line) follows the pattern of warning escalation: the first two warnings are rescinded; the third is definitive. This narrative structure teaches that prophetic intercession is effective but not unlimited — there is a point of no return that the third vision reaches.
7.7: 'This is what he showed me: behold, the Lord was standing beside a wall built with a plumb line, with a plumb line in his hand.' The plumb line (anakh) is a weight on a string used by builders to verify that a wall is perfectly vertical — a standard construction tool in the ancient world. Stone walls built without a plumb line are vulnerable to collapse; a wall 'made with a plumb line' is one that was built correctly. YHWH standing beside such a wall with a plumb line is the image of a building inspector about to test whether the structure meets its original specification — i.e., whether Israel meets the covenant standard.
7.8: 'And the LORD said to me, Amos, what do you see? And I said, A plumb line. Then the Lord said, Behold, I am setting a plumb line in the midst of my people Israel; I will never again pass by them.' The direct dialogue between YHWH and prophet is characteristic of the vision reports. 'I will never again pass by them' (lo osif od avor lo) — the decisive break from the first two visions: no more relenting, no more passing over judgment. The phrase 'pass by' (avor) may echo the Passover/Exodus language (YHWH 'passing by' to judge Egypt, Exod 12:12-13) — now inverted: YHWH will not pass by Israel without judgment.
7.9: 'And the high places of Isaac shall be made desolate, and the sanctuaries of Israel shall be laid waste, and I will rise against the house of Jeroboam with the sword.' 'High places of Isaac' (bamot Yitzchaq) — the local worship sites associated with the patriarchal tradition (cf. Gen 26:24-25: Isaac building an altar at Beersheba). 'Sanctuaries of Israel' (miqdeshei Yisrael) — the royal cult centers including Bethel and Dan. The specific mention of Jeroboam's house: the word of YHWH through Amos against Jeroboam II reaches the ears of Amaziah the priest (v10), who reports it as sedition. This was not mere religious controversy but a political threat claim.
7.10: 'Then Amaziah the priest of Bethel sent to Jeroboam king of Israel, saying, Amos has conspired against you in the midst of the house of Israel. The land is not able to bear all his words.' The Amaziah confrontation is the only extended narrative in Amos. Amaziah was the 'priest of Bethel' (kohen Bet-El) — the head of the royal cult center established by Jeroboam I (1 Kings 12:29-33). His report to King Jeroboam II frames Amos's prophecy as political conspiracy (qesher) — the term used for treasonous palace coups (2 Kings 15:10-15). In the ANE, a prophet who predicted the death of a king was legally dangerous — Jer 26:11 shows Jeremiah later facing death for similar prophecies.
7.11: 'For thus Amos has said, Jeroboam shall die by the sword, and Israel must go into exile away from his land.' Amaziah paraphrases Amos's message accurately (cf. 7:9), and the political charge is clear: predicting the king's violent death was tantamount to planning it in ancient Near Eastern legal thinking. Kings routinely consulted prophets for legitimation; a prophet who predicted their downfall was functionally an enemy of the state. The historical record: Jeroboam II did not die by the sword (2 Kings 14:29 records he 'slept with his fathers'), though his son Zechariah was assassinated after only six months (2 Kings 15:10).
7.12: 'And Amaziah said to Amos, O seer, go, flee away to the land of Judah, and eat bread there, and prophesy there.' 'Seer' (chozeh) rather than 'prophet' (nabhi) — the distinction may be dismissive or technical. Amaziah's command to 'eat bread' in Judah is a reference to prophetic income: professional prophets received payment (cf. 1 Sam 9:7-8: paying Saul a fee before consulting Samuel). Amaziah assumes Amos is a professional prophet who has come to Bethel for income and can be sent back to his own territory. This misunderstanding provides the occasion for Amos's most famous self-identification.
7.13: 'But never again prophesy at Bethel, for it is the king's sanctuary, and it is a temple of the kingdom.' 'The king's sanctuary' (miqdash-melek) and 'temple of the kingdom' (beit mamlakhah) — Amaziah reveals Bethel's actual status: it is a royal chapel, not an independent religious institution. The Bethel sanctuary was founded specifically to prevent the northern kingdom's subjects from going to Jerusalem (1 Kings 12:27-28); it was architecturally and institutionally linked to royal power. Amaziah's authority to expel Amos derives from the sanctuary's royal patronage, not from religious principle.
7.14: 'Then Amos answered and said to Amaziah, I was no prophet, nor a prophet's son, but I was a herdsman and a dresser of sycamore figs.' One of the most analyzed statements in prophetic literature. 'I was no prophet' (lo nabhi anochi) — the form could be past tense (I was not a prophet before my calling) or present (I am not a prophet in the professional sense). 'A prophet's son' (ben-nabhi) refers to the prophetic guilds (benei hanevi'im) — schools of prophets associated with Samuel (1 Sam 10:10), Elijah (2 Kings 2:3), and Elisha (2 Kings 4:38). A noqed (herdsman/sheep-breeder) — the same term used of Mesha king of Moab in 2 Kings 3:4. Sycamore fig-dresser (boles shiqmim) — sycamore figs require a specific treatment (pricking or scoring) to ripen properly.
7.15: 'But the LORD took me from following the flock, and the LORD said to me, Go, prophesy to my people Israel.' The direct divine call without mediation — 'the LORD took me' (wayyiqacheni YHWH) from the flock. This parallels David called from shepherding (2 Sam 7:8: 'I took you from the pasture, from following the sheep'), Moses called from shepherding (Exod 3:1-4), and the general OT pattern of divine call as social disruption. The call is non-institutional: no prophetic school, no mentor-figure, no royal appointment. The prophetic authority derives entirely from the divine commission, not from institutional standing.
7.16: 'Now therefore hear the word of the LORD. You say, Do not prophesy against Israel, and do not preach against the house of Isaac.' Amaziah's prohibition ('do not prophesy against Israel') is turned back on Amaziah himself — he is now the subject of a personal oracle. The phrase 'the house of Isaac' (beit Yitzchaq) echoes the 'high places of Isaac' (7:9), using the patriarchal name for the northern kingdom. The authority claimed by Amaziah to silence prophetic speech is itself the subject of prophetic judgment: the silencer of the word will hear the word pronounced against him personally.
7.17: 'Therefore thus says the LORD: Your wife shall be a prostitute in the city, and your sons and daughters shall fall by the sword, and your land shall be divided up with a measuring line; you yourself shall die in an unclean land, and Israel shall surely go into exile away from its land.' The oracle against Amaziah is comprehensive: his wife, children, land, and his own death in a foreign (unclean) land. 'A prostitute in the city' — the seizure of a defeated city's women as spoils (cf. Isa 13:16, Zech 14:2). 'Your land shall be divided with a measuring line' — the Assyrian practice of redistributing conquered territory to settlers. 'Die in an unclean land' — dying outside the covenant land meant burial without covenant community, a profound curse.

8.1: 'This is what the Lord GOD showed me: behold, a basket of summer fruit.' The fourth vision uses a wordplay that requires knowing Hebrew. A basket of qayitz (summer fruit — figs, grapes, pomegranates at their ripest). The pun in v2: YHWH says 'the qetz (end) has come upon my people Israel.' The summer fruit is ripe — fully mature, at its peak — and so is Israel's judgment: ready for harvest. The summer fruit (qayitz) was gathered at the end of the agricultural year; the qetz (end) is the end of Israel's national existence. The vision is an acted parable of completed ripeness.
8.2: 'He said, Amos, what do you see? And I said, A basket of summer fruit. Then the LORD said to me, The end has come upon my people Israel; I will never again pass by them.' The wordplay qayitz/qetz establishes that the summer harvest is a sign of the approaching end. 'I will never again pass by them' repeats 7:8 — definitively: the period of intercession and relenting is over. The phrase recurs a second time (from 7:8) to emphasize the finality. In 7:8 it followed the plumb line vision; here it follows the summer fruit, confirming the trajectory of the last two visions toward irreversible judgment.
8.3: 'The songs of the temple shall become wailings in that day, declares the Lord GOD. So many dead bodies! They are thrown everywhere. Silence!' 'Songs of the temple' (shirot heikal) — the sacred songs of the Bethel sanctuary (which had professional musicians and elaborate worship, cf. 5:23) become funeral wailings. 'In every place they are thrown' (bekhol-maqom hishlich) — the random casting of unburied bodies everywhere, the sign of total defeat and the activation of the covenant curse (Deut 28:25-26: 'your dead bodies shall be food for all birds of the air'). 'Silence!' (has) — the command to silence in the face of overwhelming death.
8.4: 'Hear this, you who trample on the needy and bring the poor of the land to an end.' The economic oracle that follows the vision — the specific social crime that makes Israel ripe for judgment. 'Trample on the needy' (hashoa'afim ebyon) and 'bring the poor to an end' (lashbit aniyei-aretz) — both verbs are strong: trampling/panting after the poor, and actively eliminating the poor class (by absorbing their land, driving them into debt slavery). This directly indicts the landholding elite of Jeroboam II's prosperous kingdom.
8.5: 'Saying, When will the new moon be over, that we may sell grain? And the Sabbath, that we may offer wheat for sale, that we may make the ephah small and the shekel great and deal deceitfully with false balances.' The religious calendar — new moon (rosh chodesh) and Sabbath (shabbat) — were both days of commercial rest (cf. 2 Kings 4:23: 'Why will you go to him today? It is neither new moon nor sabbath'). The merchants observe the form (they cease trading on holy days) while their hearts are on the resumption of fraud. The double fraud: a small ephah (short measure for the buyer) and a large shekel (overweight payment demand from the seller) simultaneously extracted maximum profit from every transaction.
8.6: 'That we may buy the poor for silver and the needy for a pair of sandals and sell the trash of the wheat?' Three practices condemned: (1) buying the poor as slaves for debt (silver), (2) buying the needy for a pair of sandals (either a pun on legal custom — sandal exchange in property transfer, Ruth 4:7 — or meaning they will enslave someone for a trivial debt), (3) selling the refuse of the wheat (maphel bar) — the chaff, husks, and dirt swept up from the threshing floor, mixed with grain and sold as if it were pure wheat. The three practices together represent systematic fraud targeting the vulnerable.
8.7: 'The LORD has sworn by the pride of Jacob: Surely I will never forget any of their deeds.' 'The pride of Jacob' (ge'on Ya'aqov) — used in 6:8 for what YHWH abhors in Israel's self-regard; here it is a divine oath title (YHWH's own pride/majesty). The paradox is that the same term applies both to the condemned pride of Israel's elite and to the divine glory that is the basis of the oath. 'I will never forget any of their deeds' — the divine memory as guarantee of judgment. In Deut 32:34 YHWH says their punishment is 'sealed up in my treasuries'; here the explicit promise is that economic oppression will not be forgotten.
8.8: 'Shall not the land tremble on this account, and everyone mourn who dwells in it, and all of it rise like the Nile, and be tossed about and sink again, like the Nile of Egypt?' The land mourning (avela ha-aretz) — the covenant land responding physically to moral catastrophe, as in Hos 4:3 and Joel 1:10. The Nile flood metaphor (ke'or Mitzrayim) — the Nile's seasonal flooding, which rises dramatically and then falls, is used as an image of land upheaval. The Nile's flood was both the source of Egyptian agricultural abundance and a powerful natural force; its application to the land of Israel suggests a catastrophic inundation of the covenant land.
8.9: 'And on that day, declares the Lord GOD, I will make the sun go down at noon and darken the earth in broad daylight.' 'The sun going down at noon' (vehevati hashemesh batzoharayim) — darkness at noon is the image of total disorientation; noon is the height of light, not the time for darkness. Whether this refers to a solar eclipse (a solar eclipse was visible in Assyria in 763 BCE and may have been seen in the Levant) or is purely metaphorical is debated. The image recurs in Joel 2:10, 3:15 and becomes the sign of the Day of YHWH. Mark 15:33 records darkness from noon to three at the crucifixion — an event the NT reads against this background.
8.10: 'I will turn your feasts into mourning and all your songs into lamentation; I will bring sackcloth on every waist and baldness on every head; I will make it like the mourning for an only son and the end of it like a bitter day.' The festival-to-mourning reversal: the three pilgrimage feasts (chagim) and their songs become funeral rites. 'Baldness on every head' (qorchah al-kol-rosh) was a mourning practice (Isa 22:12, Jer 16:6, Mic 1:16) — shaving the head as a sign of grief. 'Mourning for an only son' (evel yachid) — the most extreme grief, the death of the sole heir, the end of the family line. Jer 6:26 and Zech 12:10 use the same image.
8.11: 'Behold, the days are coming, declares the Lord GOD, when I will send a famine on the land — not a famine of bread, nor a thirst for water, but of hearing the words of the LORD.' The famine of the word (ra'av lishmo'a et divrei YHWH) — one of Amos's most distinctive and influential oracles. Having rejected the prophetic word, Israel will find itself in a situation where they desperately seek it and cannot find it. The word of YHWH, freely available and constantly refused, will be removed. The relationship between prophetic rejection and prophetic silence is explicit: 'they would not listen' (2:12, 7:12-13) leads to 'they shall seek the word of the LORD and not find it.'
8.12: 'They shall wander from sea to sea, and from north to east; they shall run to and fro, to seek the word of the LORD, but they shall not find it.' The frantic, undirected search — 'from sea to sea' (miyam ad-yam) and 'from north to east' (rather than the expected north to south) suggesting disorientation.
